content.text: Object-Oriented Programming: Classes, Objects, and the OOP Paradigm
Object-oriented programming (OOP) is a way of organizing code around real-world concepts — grouping related data and behavior together into reusable blueprints called

. Instead of writing a long list of instructions that the computer runs from top to bottom, OOP lets you model your program as a collection of interacting "things," each with its own properties and capabilities.
is a blueprint or template that describes what a thing
is a specific instance created from that blueprint — a concrete version of it with real values.
Think of a class like a cookie cutter, and an object like an actual cookie. The cutter defines the shape; each cookie is a real, individual thing made from that shape.

# This is a class — a blueprint
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed

    def bark(self):
        return f"{self.name} says: Woof!"

# These are objects — real instances
dog1 = Dog("Rex", "German Shepherd")
dog2 = Dog("Bella", "Labrador")

print(dog1.bark())  # Rex says: Woof!
print(dog2.bark())  # Bella says: Woof!
are two separate objects. They were made from the same class, but they have different data (names and breeds).
Before OOP, programs were written mostly as long sequences of functions and variables scattered across files. As programs grew larger, this became very hard to manage. OOP solves this by:
— data and the functions that work on that data live in the same place
— you define a class once and create as many objects as you need
— by encapsulating data inside objects, you control how it is accessed and changed

, OOP is especially important. AI systems have many interacting components — models, tokenizers, data loaders, training loops, agents, tools — and OOP gives you a structured way to design, extend, and swap out these components cleanly.
OOP is built on four fundamental ideas. Understanding all four is essential to writing good object-oriented code.
to that data from the outside.
Instead of having a loose variable
floating around that anyone can accidentally modify, you wrap it inside a
class and provide controlled ways to interact with it.
field is "protected" — the underscore signals that it should not be accessed directly from outside the class. You interact with it only through
. This prevents accidental corruption of data.
) means "treat as private by convention." A double underscore (
) triggers Python's name-mangling feature, making it harder (but not impossible) to access from outside.
the properties and methods of another class. This avoids repeating code and lets you build specialized versions of general concepts.
The class being inherited from is called the
(or base class, or superclass). The class that inherits is called the

Rex says: Woof!
Whiskers says: Meow!
Buddy says: Woof!
method to behave differently. This is
— a child class provides its own version of a method defined in the parent.
You can also call the parent's method using
Polymorphism means "many forms." In OOP, it means that
different classes can be used through the same interface
, even though they behave differently internally.
In the animal example above, you can call
on any animal object without knowing whether it is a
. The right method gets called automatically. This makes code flexible and extensible.

def make_noise(animal):
    print(animal.speak())  # works for any Animal subclass

make_noise(Dog("Rex"))       # Rex says: Woof!
make_noise(Cat("Whiskers"))  # Whiskers says: Meow!
This is powerful because you can add a new animal class (
will work with it automatically — without changing the function itself.
in Python extends this further: if an object has the method being called, Python doesn't care what class it belongs to.

of how something works and exposing only what the user needs to know. You use a microwave without knowing how electromagnetic radiation heats food — the interface is simple even if the internals are complex.
In Python, abstraction is often implemented using
every shape must be able to do (calculate area and perimeter), but not
. Each subclass provides its own implementation. You can't accidentally create a bare
object — Python will raise an error.
A class typically has these parts:
Runs when an object is created. Sets up its initial state.
Refers to the specific object being acted on.
Works on the class itself via
A utility function scoped inside the class
Defines how the object looks when printed
In real programs, objects don't exist in isolation — they collaborate. There are two main ways objects relate to each other:
. Rather than inheriting from Engine, Car
Composition is generally preferred over deep inheritance chains.
Deep inheritance (5+ levels) makes code hard to follow and debug. Composition keeps things modular and swappable.
— to let your objects work with Python's built-in operators and functions.
OOP in Generative AI: Real-World Example
Modern AI frameworks are built almost entirely on OOP. Here is how it shows up in practice.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleClassifier(nn.Module):  # inherits from PyTorch's base class
    def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size, num_classes):
        super().__init__()  # initializes nn.Module internals
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden_size)
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):  # called when you run model(x)
        x = self.fc1(x)
        x = self.relu(x)
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x

model = SimpleClassifier(input_size=784, hidden_size=128, num_classes=10)
Every PyTorch model is a class that inherits from
to define how data flows through your network. PyTorch handles the rest (backpropagation, parameter tracking, etc.) through the parent class.
class with specialized subclasses — is exactly how frameworks like LangChain and AutoGen structure AI agent tools.
OOP is a good fit when:
Your problem naturally maps to "things" with state and behavior (users, accounts, products, agents, models)
You need to create many similar objects with different data
You want to build reusable, extensible components
Your codebase is large enough that organization and structure become important
You are using frameworks (PyTorch, Django, FastAPI) that expect OOP patterns
The task is simple and linear (a short script to rename files)
You are doing pure data transformation (often better handled with functional programming)
Overhead of designing class hierarchies outweighs the benefit
Trying to model everything with deep class hierarchies makes code rigid. Prefer composition when possible.
as its first parameter. Forgetting it causes a

class Broken:
    def greet():  # missing self
        return "Hello"

b = Broken()
b.greet()  # TypeError: greet() takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given
Never use a mutable object (like a list or dict) as a default argument:
4. Treating class variables like instance variables
A class variable is shared across all instances. Modifying it on one instance affects all of them if you're not careful.
When just starting, it is often better to write plain functions first and refactor into classes when the structure becomes clear. Do not force OOP onto every problem.
A class should represent one concept. If a class is doing too many unrelated things, split it.
Build complex objects by combining simple ones, not by creating deep hierarchies.
It makes debugging vastly easier — you can see the object's state just by printing it.
It should set up state, not do heavy computation or I/O.
Especially in AI projects where classes represent complex concepts.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float
    label: str = "origin"

p = Point(3.0, 4.0, "A")
print(p)  # Point(x=3.0, y=4.0, label='A')
Modeling complex systems with state and behavior
In practice, Python supports all three styles. Most real AI projects mix OOP (for model architecture and agent design) with functional style (for data preprocessing and transformations).
Think of a class as a

it makes. The factory (class) has a blueprint — it knows how to create a product with certain features and behaviors. Each product (object) is independent, has its own data, and can perform its own actions. Multiple factories can be built on top of each other (inheritance), or a factory can use other factory products as components (composition).
, you are running the factory, and
is the product that comes out — a fully formed object with its own name, breed, and the ability to bark.
is a real instance created from that blueprint
The four pillars of OOP are
is the constructor — it sets up an object's initial state when it is created
refers to the specific object a method is acting on
lets child classes reuse and extend parent class behavior;
, etc.) let your classes integrate with Python's built-in operators
In Generative AI, OOP is foundational — frameworks like PyTorch and LangChain are built entirely on classes and inheritance
